coredata.dc:title: The facile detection and micromechanism of ATMP and DTPMP by fluorescence sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon nanomaterials
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Xingen
Feng, Shasha
Lei, Wu
Xia, Mingzhu
Wang, Fengyun
Ni, Yue
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, the N-doped fluorescent carbon nanomaterials (NCMs) were prepared. Fe3+ quenched the fluorescence of NCMs effectively, while the addition of different concentrations of ATMP or DTPMP can restore the fluorescence intensity of NCMs to different degrees. Therefore, the Fe-NCMs fluorescent probe can quantitatively detect ATMP or DTPMP, and the detection limit (LOD) of ATMP and DTPMP was 0.479 μΜ and 0.254 μΜ, respectively. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of NCMs by Fe3+ was revealed by UV–vis absorption spectra and the fluorescence lifetime analysis, the results showed that the fluorescence quenching was a combination of static and dynamic quenching. Moreover, based on Density Function Theory (DFT) calculation, basin analysis, topology analysis of atoms in molecules (AIM), electron localization function (ELF) analysis, and bond order density (BOD) analysis were firstly combined to reveal the difference in fluorescence restoration degree between the two organic phosphonates on Fe-NCMs. The detection mechanism of ATMP and DTPMP by Fe-NCMs was explained from a microscopic point of view.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon nanomaterials
Fluorescence sensor
Organic phosphonates
Quenching mechanism
Quantum chemical calculation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The quantitative detection of ATMP and DTPMP
The stability and anti-interference ability of Fe-NCMs fluorescence sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (52072180, 51872140) financially support all the research work in this group.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The structural formulas (a-ATMP, c-DTPMP) and optimal configurations (b-ATMP, d-DTPMP) (C-tan, O-red, H-white, N-blue, P-gray), the schematic illustration of the preparation process of NCMs (e).
The FTIR (a) and enlarged FTIR (b) of NCMs and Fe-NCMs, the SEM of NCMs (c) and Fe-NCMs (d).

The XPS survey spectra (a), high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s, C1s, and O1s of NCMs (b–d); the 3D fluorescence spectra (e) and contour (f) of NCMs; the ratio of different elements in XPS (g), the UV absorption spectrum, the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of NCMs (h).

The fluorescence quenching response of different metal ions to NCMs (a, F0-the fluorescence intensity of NCMs, F-the fluorescence intensity of NCMs after adding different metal ions with the same concentration), the fluorescence spectra of Fe-NCMs at different Fe3+ concentrations (b), the effect of Fe3+ concentration on the fluorescence intensity of NCMs (c), the fluorescence quenching effect of 0.48 mM Fe3+on NCMs (d), the effect of different Fe3+ concentration on the fluorescence recovery of NCMs by ATMP or DTPMP (e, F- the fluorescence intensity of Fe-NCMs after adding ATMP or DTPMP, F0-the fluorescence intensity of Fe-NCMs), the effect of solution pH on the fluorescence quenching of Fe-NCMs (f, F0-the fluorescence intensity of NCMs, F-the fluorescence intensity of Fe-NCMs at the same pH).
The fluorescence response of Fe-NCMs with different concentrations of ATMP (a) and DTPMP (c), the linear relationship between F/F0 of Fe-NCMs and the concentration of ATMP (b) and DTPMP (d).

The fluorescence recovery of NCMs with 110 μM ATMP or DTPMP (a), the fluorescence stability of NCMs and Fe-NCMs (b), the effect of ion intensity on fluorescence intensity of NCMs and Fe-NCMs (c), the anti-interference ability of Fe-NCMs (d).

The UV–vis adsorption spectrum of Fe3+ and the emission spectrum of NCMs (a), the fluorescence lifetime at different Fe3+ concentrations (b), Stern-Volmer curve of Fe-NCMs at different Fe3+ concentrations (c), the effect of Fe3+ concentration on the UV adsorption of NCMs (d).
The colored ESP (the unit of ESP is kcal/mol) isosurface and minimum points of ATMP (a) and DTPMP (b), the AIM topology analysis of ATMP-Fe (c) and DTPMP-Fe (d).

The shaded surface map with projection effect of ELF of ATMP-Fe (a) and DTPMP-Fe (b), the BOD isosurface (c-ATMP-Fe, d-DTPMP-Fe, isosurface = 0.05, (C-tan, O-red, H-white, N-blue, P-gray, Fe-pink), the color-filled map of BOD (e-ATMP-Fe, f-DTPMP-Fe).
The fluorescence lifetime at different Fe3+ concentration.
Determination of ATMP and DTPMP in actual sample.

head.ce:title: The facile detection and micromechanism of ATMP and DTPMP by fluorescence sensor based on nitrogen-doped carbon nanomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanjing Electronic Devices Institute, Nanjing, 210014, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanjing Electronic Devices Institute, Nanjing, 210014, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, the N-doped fluorescent carbon nanomaterials (NCMs) were prepared. Fe3+ quenched the fluorescence of NCMs effectively, while the addition of different concentrations of ATMP or DTPMP can restore the fluorescence intensity of NCMs to different degrees. Therefore, the Fe-NCMs fluorescent probe can quantitatively detect ATMP or DTPMP, and the detection limit (LOD) of ATMP and DTPMP was 0.479 μΜ and 0.254 μΜ, respectively. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of NCMs by Fe3+ was revealed by UV–vis absorption spectra and the fluorescence lifetime analysis, the results showed that the fluorescence quenching was a combination of static and dynamic quenching. Moreover, based on Density Function Theory (DFT) calculation, basin analysis, topology analysis of atoms in molecules (AIM), electron localization function (ELF) analysis, and bond order density (BOD) analysis were firstly combined to reveal the difference in fluorescence restoration degree between the two organic phosphonates on Fe-NCMs. The detection mechanism of ATMP and DTPMP by Fe-NCMs was explained from a microscopic point of view.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) are a new kind of nanoscale materials [1]. According to the size difference, these CNMs can be divided into zero-dimensional nano-points, one-dimensional nanotubes, two-dimensional, and three-dimensional materials [2,3]. CNMs have little harm to organisms and are low-cost. In addition, its advantages of good biocompatibility, high chemical stability, and adjustable luminescence [4] have attracted much attention in many fields including electronics [5], catalysis [6], analysis and detection [7–10], energy conversion [11], biosensing [12], and imaging [13]. The surface of CNMs is rich in carbon-based functional groups, which can interact with different kinds of analytes through π-π stacking, electrostatic interaction, and hydrogen bonding [14].

CNMs have attracted the attention of many researchers as a luminescent material in analysis and detection, in which it is used to detect various kinds of analytes [15–18], especially metal ions [19,20]. Gangaraju G et al. [21] reported the green synthesis of carbon dots from prawn shells for detecting copper ions (Cu2+), and the carbon dots exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity toward Cu2+ with a low detection limit of 5 nM.

In general, carbon-only CNMs lack active sites due to their simple composition, which limits their application. Therefore, during the actual synthesis process, different metals or non-metals have been doped into CNMs. Nitrogen, sulfur, boron, and other elements are usually doped into CNMs to improve their internal structure and electron distribution, to obtain a higher quantum yield, and better luminescence performance [22,23]. Nitrogen is a very effective doping element [24]. Atchudan R et al. [25] used Chionanthus retusus fruit extract to prepare N-doped carbon dots, and the N–CDs demonstrated highly durable fluorescence properties and low cytotoxicity with a quantum yield of 9%. Jie S et al. [26] used N, S-co-doped carbon dots (N, SCDs) from biomass to be the sensitive probes for Cr (VI) detection. However, the application of CNMs as fluorescent probes in the detection of organic phosphonates is rarely studied.

Organic phosphonates are widely used in many fields because of their special properties, such as printing and dyeing [27], detergent [28], industrial circulating cooling water systems [29,30], etc. They are common in wastewater, especially in industrial wastewater and circulating cooling water systems [31]. Due to its strong chelating ability with metal ions, it will affect the migration and removal of heavy metal ions, and result in the accumulation of heavy metal ions in the environment [32], increasing the pressure on the ecological environment. After material circulation in nature, especially under the condition of light, phosphonates are more likely to be transformed into ammethylphosphonic acid (AMPA), which is more toxic and threatens environmental safety [33].

Because of the low reactivity of most organic phosphates, their low selectivity to rectifying columns, and the absence of characteristic UV and IR signals, their analysis is rather difficult [34]. Klinger applied an HPLC-MS method for the determination of phosphonates, but this method could be satisfactorily used for demineralized water only [35]. B. Nowack [36] developed a new method of ion-pair high-performance liquid chromatography for the detection of four organic phosphonates, including amino trimethylene phosphonic acid (ATMP), 1-hydroxyethylidene-1,1-diphosphonic acid (HEDP), diethylenetriamine pentamethylene phosphonic acid (DTPMP) and ethylenediaminetetra (methylenephosphonic acid (EDTMP). After separation by chromatographic column, the concentration of the target detected substance could be detected in about 20 min, and the limits of detection were 0.05 μΜ for ATMP and EDTMP, 0.1 μΜ for DTPMP, and 0.5 μΜ for HEDP. Although this method has a low detection limit, the pretreatment process is complex and time-consuming. Dominic Armbruster [37] combined anion exchange chromatography-electrospray ionization-time of flight mass spectrometry (ESI-TOF) with inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) to detect a variety of organic phosphonates. However, this method of detection requires the determination of total phosphorus content by ICP coupled emission spectroscopy, which is a complex process, and the combined use of multiple instruments undoubtedly increased the cost of detection. Therefore, it is very necessary to develop a simple and effective detection method for the standard discharge of organic phosphonates.

ATMP and DTPMP are widely used as scale inhibitors and corrosion inhibitors in circulating water treatment [38,39]. In our previous work [40], we prepared a Cu-MOF-74 fluorescent probe to detect ATMP, and the LOD was 0.844 μM. Moreover, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on quercetin (QC) and divalent zinc ion (Zn2+) to comparatively detect ATMP, HEDP, and DTPMP and the detection limits (LODs) were 0.223, 0.205, and 0.152 μM, respectively [41]. But we did not probe the difference in fluorescence quenching degree between the three organic phosphonates on Zn-QC to reveal the micromechanism. To our best knowledge, few reports were found on the application of CNMs for the detection of organic phosphonates. Therefore, in the present work, we used N-doped CNMs and Fe3+ ions to construct a fluorescence detector for quantitative detection of ATMP and DTPMP for the first time, aiming to explore the possible application of CNMs-based fluorescence detector in the detection of low concentration organic phosphonates. The fluorescence intensity of nitrogen-doped carbon nanomaterials (NCMs) was significantly reduced after modification with Fe3+. The addition of ATMP and DTPMP can effectively restore the fluorescence intensity of NCMs. Moreover, we applied Multiwfn wavefunction analysis program [42] to study the affinity between ATMP, DTPMP, and Fe3+ ion at the atomic scale through basin analysis of electrostatic potential (ESP), atoms in molecules (AIM) topology analysis, and electron localization function (ELF) analysis, thus revealing the difference of fluorescence restoration degree between two organic phosphonates on Fe-NCMs. The detection mechanism of ATMP and DTPMP by Fe-NCMs was explained from a microscopic point of view.

ATMP and DTPMP were obtained from Changzhou Yao Tong De Chemical Co., LTD. Citric acid monohydrate and FeCl3·6H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. All other reagents were analytical pure (AR) reagents without further purification. The structures and the optimal configurations of ATMP and DTPMP by DFT calculation were displayed in Fig. 1 .

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were taken on the JSM-IT500HR. UV–visible absorption spectra were measured on a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Seymour Fisher, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained on the Model Nexsa spectrometer (Thermo Fisher). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded by using a Nicolet IS-10 (Thermo Fisher) infrared spectrometer. All photoluminescence spectra were measured by an FL3-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Jobin Yvon, France).

Preparation of buffer system: A certain volume of 0.2 M NaOH solution was mixed with 10 mL phosphoric acid, boric acid, and acetic acid solution (0.04 M) to prepare the Britton-Robison buffer system (BR buffer solution) uniformly [43].

Preparation of NCMs: NCMs with strong fluorescence were prepared by referring to the reported synthesis method [44]. 1.261 g (0.01 mol) of melamine was added to 25 mL of deionized water and stirred for 20 min. Then 1.050 g (0.005 mol) of citric acid monohydrate was added and the mixture was stirred for another 10 min. Then the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL high-pressure reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the initial sample was cooled to room temperature naturally, and the obtained sample was ultrasonic for 30 min to make it completely distributed in the solution. The sample was centrifuged at 8000 rpm/min for 30 min to collect the supernatant, which was diluted with deionized water and used as NCMs reserve solution.

ATMP and DTPMP were dissolved in deionized water to prepare an organic phosphonate standard reserve solution (1 mM). Fe-NCMs fluorescent probe solution was constructed by adding a certain volume of Fe3+ solution (10 μM) into 2 mL NCMs reserve solution, and the concentration of Fe3+ was 0–0.48 mM, the pH of the system was adjusted with BR buffer solution. After the solution was evenly mixed and stabilized, the fluorescence emission spectra of Fe-NCMs solution were recorded to determine the optimum Fe3+ concentration and pH. Then, different volumes of organic phosphonate solutions were added to 2 mL Fe-NCMs solution (Fe3+ concentration of 0.36 mM, pH of 7.0), the fluorescence emission spectra were measured after the mixed solution was stirred evenly. The excitation wavelength was 368 nm, emission spectrum range of 385–650 nm, and the excitation and emission slit width of 3 nm for both Fe-NCMs and ATMP-Fe-NCMs system. Using lavenberg-Marquardt deconvolution method, the time-resolved photoluminescence decay curves of NCMs and Fe-NCMs were detected by fitting multiple exponential decay functions. All detection procedures were carried out at room temperature, and each experiment was repeated three times to calculate the standard deviation.

The quantum chemistry calculation and analysis of this work were implemented in Gaussian09 [45,46] with B3LYP hybrid functional combined 6-311G* basis set and the Multiwfn wavefunction program. The C, H, O, N atoms were optimized using classical density functional theory (DFT) at B3LYP/6-311G* level, while Fe3+ was optimized at Lanl2TZ level of the theory. The basin analysis, AIM topology analysis, fuzzy atomic space analysis, orbital component analysis was implemented by the main function of 17, 2, 15, and 8, respectively.

In Fig. 2 (a-b), the peaks at 3463 cm−1, 3271 cm−1, and 3078 cm−1 can be attributed to the N–H and O–H stretching vibration [47]. The three characteristic absorption peaks at 1715 cm−1,1663 cm−1 and 1612 cm−1 were caused by stretching vibration of CO in COOH and CONH [48]. In addition, the vibration absorption peaks of C–O–C, C–N and CC/CN can be observed at 1509 cm−1, 1432 cm−1, 1382 cm−1, 1184 cm−1 and 1167 cm−1 [49]. Fig. 2b showed that after modification with Fe3+, the intensity of peaks at 1715 cm−1 and 1432 cm−1 obviously weakened, while the peak intensity of 1663 cm−1 and 1382 cm−1 enhanced, which indicated the coordination between Fe3+ ions and NCMs occurred. Meanwhile, it can be seen from Fig. 2c-d that the structure of NCMs was evenly distributed in a small sphere, while the morphology of Fe-NCMs was aggregated and flaky, which was very different from that of NCMs.

XPS analysis was performed to understand the elemental composition and chemical states of the NCMs (Fig. 3 ). According to XPS full-scan spectrum, the contents of carbon (C1s, 288.52 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 399.26 eV), and oxygen (O1, 531.59 eV) in NCMs were 38.74%, 46.88%, and 14.38%, respectively, indicating that nitrogen has been successfully doped. In the XPS high-resolution spectrum of C1s, two peaks appeared near 284.28 eV and 288.58 eV corresponding to CC/C–C and CO/CN, respectively [50]. In addition, the presence of N–C–O bonds (398.78 eV) and C–N–C (399.88 eV) was confirmed by two peaks in XPS high-resolution spectra of N1s [51]. Similarly, two peaks in the spectrum of O1s were attributed to CO (531.58 eV) and C–O–C/C–OH (532.78 eV) bonds, respectively [50,52]. The fluorescence intensity of NCMs at different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3e and f) showed the best excitation wavelength was about 370 nm and has an obvious excitation dependence. In addition, with 368 nm as the excitation wavelength, the best emission peak can be observed at 449 nm. Fig. 3h showed that an absorption peak was observed at 330 nm, which may be caused by the N-π* transition of CO or CN in NCMs [53]. It can be concluded that the surface of the prepared NCMs was rich in groups containing COOH and NH2, resulting in energy differences between different surface states, thus leading to excitation dependence [54], which is conducive to further application.

The presence of carboxyl groups and amino groups makes NCMs have good hydrophilicity and the ability of complexing with metal ions. To evaluate the selectivity of NCMs solution to metal ions, the effects of 12 metal ions, including Pb2+, Cr3+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ and other 8 metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of NCMs were investigated (Fig. 4 a). Compared with other metal ions, Fe3+ has the most obvious quenching effect on NCMs, with a quenching efficiency of 72.5%. Therefore, Fe3+ was selected as the modifier of NCMs. In addition, different concentrations of Fe3+ have different quenching efficiency on the fluorescence intensity of NCMs. Fig. 4b-c showed that with the increase of Fe3+concentration, the PL intensity of NCMs gradually decreased, when the concentration of Fe3+ was 0.48 mM, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of NCMs was higher as 97.8% (Fig. 4d). Meanwhile, the quantitative detection of organic phosphonates can be achieved by competing with NCMs for Fe3+ and partially restoring the fluorescence intensity of NCMs. The concentration of organic phosphonates added to the solution is very low, too low or too high Fe3+ concentration will affect the fluorescence response of organic phosphonates to NCMs. It can be seen from Fig. 4e, the fluorescence recovery of NCMs by ATMP or DTPMP was most suitable when Fe3+ concentration was 0.36 mM. Therefore, the concentration of Fe3+ was selected as 0.36 mM in the subsequent experiments.

There are many carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of NCMs that are easily affected by the pH of the solution, so it is necessary to study the fluorescence intensity of NCMs and Fe-NCMs in solutions with different pH. As shown in Fig. 4f, with the increase of alkalinity of the solution, the fluorescence quenching efficiency showed an overall trend of first increasing and then decreasing, and reached the maximum when the solution was about neutral. The pH-dependent behavior of NCMs can result from protonation and deprotonation of chemical groups on their surface [55]. Therefore, to ensure the best detection performance, the pH of the system was kept neutral.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quantitative detection of ATMP and DTPMP
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that organic phosphonates such as ATMP and DTPMP have strong chelating ability with metal ions [56]. Therefore, it is feasible to recover the fluorescence characteristics of NCMS and quantitatively detect ATMP and DTPMP by competing with NCMS for iron ions. It was found that when different concentrations of ATMP and DTPMP were added to Fe-NCMs solution, a new equilibrium was established and the fluorescence properties of NCMs were restored to different degrees. Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5c studied the fluorescence spectra of Fe-NCMs solution when ATMP and DTPMP were added in the concentration range of 0–140 μM. With the increase of the concentration of ATMP or DTPMP, the fluorescence intensity gradually restored, and showed a positive linear relationship with the concentration of organic phosphonates within 0–110 μM, when the concentration of ATMP or DTPMP was over 110 μM, the fluorescence of the system will remain stable and unchanged. According to the detection limit algorithm (LOD = 3S/k, S denotes the standard deviation of a blank sample, K denotes the slope of the fitted calibration curve) [57], the detection limits of ATMP and DTPMP were calculated to be 0.479 μM and 0.254 μM, respectively.

The difference of the Fe-NCMs fluorescence probe on the sensing of ATMP and DTPMP was reflected in the different restore degrees of the fluorescence intensity of the system at the same dosage. Fig. 6 a showed that the restore degree of fluorescence intensity of Fe-NCMs was higher after adding DTPMP than that of ATMP, which implied Fe-NCMs had a better response to DTPMP than ATMP. DTPMP molecule may contain more phosphonic acid groups and have a stronger affinity with iron ions than ATMP. In the following study of the detection mechanism, Multiwfn wavefunction program based on density functional theory (DFT) will be executed to analyze the affinity between ATMP or DTPMP and iron ion in detail.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability and anti-interference ability of Fe-NCMs fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6b compared the fluorescence intensity changes of NCMs and Fe-NCMs solution before and after one month. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of NCMs and Fe-NCMs remained unchanged, indicating the prepared NCMs and Fe-NCMs had good stability. In addition, the changes in fluorescence intensity of NCMs and Fe-NCMs with different ion concentrations (0, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 mM) were investigated in detail in Fig. 6c. It can be told that the increase of ion concentration in the solution had no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of NCMs and Fe-NCMs fluorescent probes. In the practical environmental water sample, there are not only detected substances, but also other natural interfering substances. The existence of these substances will more or less affect the stability of the probe and the accuracy of the detection results [58]. Therefore, to evaluate the anti-interference ability of Fe-NCMs fluorescent probe in detecting ATMP and DTPMP, some common ions in water (F−, Cl−, Br−, CO3 2−, C2O4 2−, SO4 2−), organic substances (l-lysine, uric acid, l-glutamic acid) and homologated organic phosphonates (HEDP, EDTMP) were selected to be added into the system as interfering substances to investigate the changes of fluorescence intensity (Fig. 6d). When the concentration of interferents was 0.25 mM, it was obvious that they had little significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of the system. It can be seen that the prepared Fe-NCMs fluorescent probe was highly selective for the detection of ATMP and DTPMP, and has the strong anti-interference ability.

UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3+ and emission spectra of NCMs were measured (Fig. 7 a). Based on their overlap, the possibility of fluorescence quenching caused by fluorescence resonance energy transfer can be determined [59]. Fig. 7a showed that there was no overlap between the absorption peak of Fe3+ and the excitation-emission peak of NCMs, so resonance energy transfer was impossible [60].

Fluorescence lifetime is the most accurate method to measure dynamic and static quenching. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime of NCMs solution at different concentrations of Fe3+ (0, 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6 mM) was tested. Fig. 7b showed the time-resolved fluorescence spectra at different Fe3+ concentrations, and the fluorescence lifetime results were summarized in Table 1 . It can be seen that with the increase of the Fe3+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity reduced and the fluorescence lifetime of the excited state of the system was shortened. The fluorescence lifetime of NCMs solution was 5.36 ns without the addition of Fe3+. However, the fluorescence lifetime was shortened to 0.72, 0.68 and 0.70 ns after adding Fe3+of 0.2, 0.4 and 0.6 mM, respectively. The shortening of fluorescence lifetime proved that dynamic quenching was involved in the fluorescence quenching process.

In some quenching systems, dynamic quenching and static quenching mechanisms exist at the same time. Therefore, to further explore whether static quenching was involved in the quenching of NCMs by Fe3+, the Stern-Volmer curve of Fe-NCMs was studied (Fig. 7c). From the nonlinear characteristics of the Stern-Volmer curve, it indicated that there is a static quenching mechanism between NCMs and Fe3+ [61,62].

Meanwhile, the absorption spectra of NCMs at different Fe3+ concentrations were recorded (Fig. 7d). With the increase of Fe3+ concentration (0–0.15 mM), the intensity of the UV absorption peak was enhanced, and the absorption peak at 233 nm has a slight blue shift (Δλ = 6 nm), which proved that static quenching was involved in the fluorescence quenching process of NCMs by Fe3+. Therefore, Fig. 7a-d implied the fluorescence quenching of NCMs by Fe3+ was a combination of static and dynamic quenching.

The more negative the electrostatic potential on the molecular surface, the more likely the corresponding atom is to provide electrons for electrophilic reaction [63]. Fig. 8 (a-b) showed that the ESP minimum points of ATMP or DTPMP were located around O atoms of the phosphonic acid group, which indicated phosphonic acid oxygen atoms tend to contribute lone pair electrons to coordinate with an iron atom. It also can be seen that the three phosphonic groups of ATMP were equivalent, while one of the phosphonic groups in DTPMP had the lowest ESP than that of ATMP and was easier prone to electrophilic reactions.

In AIM theory, the bond critical point (BCP) is considered to be the most representative point in the interatomic interaction region, so the properties of BCP can be used to investigate the characteristics of the corresponding chemical bond, including strength and nature. The electron density ρ(BCP) and potential energy density V(BCP) at BCP position is closely related to the strength of the chemical bond. For a similar chemical bond, the greater ρ(BCP) and the more negative V(BCP), the greater the strength of the chemical bond. Meanwhile, Energy density (H(r)) is the energy of an electron at a certain point, H(BCP) < 0 can be considered covalent [64], and the physical significance of H(BCP)/ρ(BCP) is the energy density of the unit electron at the BCP position, which is called Bond Degree (BD), for a covalent bond, H(BCP) < 0, the more negative BD is, the stronger the interaction is [65].

Fig. 8c-d indicated that the BCPs of O–Fe bonds in ATMP-Fe or DTPMP-Fe had the characteristics of covalent bonds: ρ(BCP) > 0, V(BCP) < 0, H (BCP) < 0, indicating the formation of O–Fe covalent bonds. Moreover, by comparing ATMP-Fe and DTPMP-Fe, it can be known that ρ(BCP) of the O–Fe bond of DTPMP-Fe was obviously higher than that of ATMP-Fe, while both V(BCP) and BD values were more negative than that of ATMP-Fe, illustrating that the bond strength of O–Fe of the former was higher than that of the latter, which was the cause of fluorescence restoration efficiency of Fe-NCMs by DTPMP was higher than that of ATMP.

ELF analysis (Fig. 9 a and b) showed that the electrons were greatly localized around O atoms of ATMP or DTPMP, clearly rendering the covalent bond formed between O atoms and Fe atoms. Meanwhile, the bond order density (BOD) [66] was introduced to vividly visualize the existence of O–Fe covalent bonds and the difference between ATMP-Fe and DTPMP-Fe. In Fig. 9c-d, it can be seen that the aggregation of electrons density on phosphonate oxygen atoms, which further confirmed that the formation of the O–Fe bond was due to phosphonate oxygen atoms of ATMP or DTPMP providing lone pair electrons for iron coordination.

Furthermore, Fig. 9e-f showed that the large contribution region was only distributed on the O–Fe bond axis, and was basically the contribution of oxygen atoms, which was in accordance with the results of Fig. 9a-b. Especially, the BOD strength of the two O–Fe bonds of ATMP-Fe was similar, while it was obviously different for DTPMP-Fe, and the BOD of one O–Fe bond was stronger than that of ATMP-Fe (the red region around one O atom), which was agreement with the results of AIM topology analysis.

According to the above comprehensive analysis, it implied that the phosphonic acid oxygen atoms of ATMP and DTPMP can provide lone pair electrons to coordinate with iron ions to form stable complexes and release NCMs, so as to restore the fluorescence of NCMs and achieve quantitative detection of ATMP and DTPMP. In addition, the coordination bond strength between DTPMP and iron was stronger than that of ATMP, so DTPMP can restore the fluorescence NCM to a higher degree than ATMP, which was consistent with the experimental results.

The label recovery experiment was carried out to explore the practicability of the prepared fluorescent probe for actual sample analysis. Quantitative ATMP or DTPMP was added to actual water sample (Table 2 ). According to the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and ATMP or DTPMP concentration, the recovery rate was calculated as higher as 99.34%–103.11%, 99.09%–103.85%, respectively, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 1.43%–3.26%, 1.29%–2.67%, respectively. Meanwhile, to compare the most conventional detection method of organic phosphonate in chemical laboratory-phosphomolybdenum blue spectrophotometry [67] was also applied to detect the actual samples. The results showed that for the detection of ATMP (0.89 μM) and DTPMP (1.02 μM), the R.S.D value was as high as 62.3% and 37.4%, which was far lower than the accuracy of the fluorescence detection method. Therefore, Fe-NCMs fluorescent sensor has good reproducibility and can satisfactorily detect ATMP and DTPMP in actual samples.

Fe3+ can effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of NCMs, and the fluorescence quenching was a combination of static and dynamic quenching. The addition of ATMP or DTPMP can restore the fluorescence intensity of NCMs to different degrees, in the concentration range of 0–110 μM, the detection limits of ATMP and DTPMP were 0.479 and 0.254 μM, respectively, which makes it possible for NCMs to be used for the quantitative and sensitive detection of water-soluble phosphonates in the environment. Multiwfn wavefunction analysis revealed that the phosphonate groups of ATMP and DTPMP have the lowest electrostatic potential, the oxygen atoms of phosphonate provided lone pair electrons to coordinate with iron atom, and the coordination bond strength of O–Fe by DTPMP and iron atom was higher than that of ATMP. Therefore, DTPMP has a higher restoration of NCMs fluorescence intensity than that ATMP.

Xingen Xie: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shasha Feng: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Wu Lei: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Mingzhu Xia: Software, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Fengyun Wang: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, All authors read and approved the manuscript. Yue Ni: Analysis of samples, fluorescence, performance test and some data processing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Natural Science Foundation of China (52072180, 51872140) financially support all the research work in this group.
